mechanism: Additive anticoagulation + platelet inhibition
management: Avoid combination unless benefit proven (e.g. mechanical valve); use PPI for GI protection

mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits CYP2C9 → warfarin metabolism reduced → INR increases
management: Reduce warfarin dose 35-65%. Monitor INR twice weekly for 4 weeks.

mechanism: Fluconazole strongly inhibits CYP2C9 → warfarin AUC increases 2-4x
management: Reduce warfarin dose ~50%. Daily INR monitoring during fluconazole course.

mechanism: Metronidazole inhibits CYP2C9 + reduces intestinal vitamin K
management: Monitor INR frequently; expect warfarin dose reduction.

mechanism: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2 component of warfarin metabolism; also reduces gut vitamin K synthesis
management: Monitor INR 2-3 days after starting ciprofloxacin and after stopping.

mechanism: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 (minor warfarin pathway) + reduces gut flora vitamin K
management: Monitor INR closely during course.

mechanism: Clarithromycin MBI of CYP3A4 → simvastatin exposure increases 8-10x → rhabdomyolysis
management: CONTRAINDICATED. Stop simvastatin during clarithromycin course. Switch to pravastatin.

mechanism: CYP3A4 MBI → atorvastatin AUC increases 4-5x
management: Use lowest atorvastatin dose (10mg) or switch to pravastatin/rosuvastatin.

mechanism: Amiodarone CYP3A4 inhibition → atorvastatin AUC increases 2-3x
management: Limit atorvastatin to 40mg/day. Monitor for myopathy.

mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition by amiodarone + P-gp inhibition → simvastatin 6x increase
management: CONTRAINDICATED >20mg simvastatin with amiodarone. Switch to pravastatin.

mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits P-gp and CYP3A4 → digoxin AUC increases 50-100%
management: Reduce digoxin dose 50% when starting amiodarone. Monitor levels closely.

mechanism: Clarithromycin inhibits P-gp → reduced digoxin renal/intestinal secretion → toxicity
management: Monitor digoxin levels. Consider dose reduction.

mechanism: Contrast nephropathy → AKI → metformin accumulates → lactic acidosis
management: Hold metformin 24-48hr before IV contrast; resume 48hr after if Cr stable.

mechanism: Furosemide may increase metformin AUC by reducing tubular secretion
management: Monitor renal function. High furosemide doses may worsen dehydration → lactic acidosis risk.

mechanism: Both cause K+ retention → additive hyperkalemia risk
management: Monitor K+ weekly for 4 weeks, then monthly. Target K+ <5.5 mEq/L.

mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandins → blunt ACEi antihypertensive effect + AKI risk
management: Avoid NSAIDs if possible. Monitor BP and renal function closely.

mechanism: Additive serotonergic effect + CYP2D6 inhibition impairs tramadol metabolism → serotonin syndrome
management: Avoid combination. If necessary, use lowest doses and monitor for serotonin syndrome signs.

mechanism: Fluoxetine is a strong CYP2D6 inhibitor → metoprolol levels increase 4-5x
management: Reduce metoprolol dose significantly. Monitor HR and BP. Consider beta-blocker without CYP2D6 metabolism.

mechanism: Calcium forms insoluble chelate with levothyroxine in GI tract → reduced absorption
management: Separate by AT LEAST 4 hours (levothyroxine first, then calcium 4hr+ later).

mechanism: PPIs reduce gastric acid → less ionization of levothyroxine → reduced absorption
management: Take levothyroxine 30-60min before omeprazole. Monitor TSH after PPI initiation.

mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9 → phenytoin levels increase → toxicity
management: Monitor phenytoin levels closely. May need 30-50% dose reduction.

mechanism: Phenytoin induces CYP2C9 → warfarin metabolism accelerated. Then phenytoin also displaces warfarin protein binding initially → complex biphasic interaction.
management: Monitor INR very closely. Interaction is unpredictable.

mechanism: Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase → 6-MP (azathioprine metabolite) cannot be inactivated → severe myelosuppression/toxicity
management: CONTRAINDICATED unless dose reduced to 25% of azathioprine dose with intensive CBC monitoring.

mechanism: Additive QT prolongation → increased torsades de pointes risk
management: Avoid combination in patients with cardiac risk factors. ECG monitoring if necessary.

mechanism: Additive QT prolongation
management: Monitor QTc. Avoid in patients with hypokalemia or baseline QTc >450ms.

mechanism: Clarithromycin MBI CYP3A4 → cyclosporine levels increase 3-5x → nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity
management: Monitor cyclosporine trough daily. Reduce cyclosporine dose 50-75%.

mechanism: Cyclosporine inhibits OATP1B1 and P-gp → atorvastatin AUC increases 8-10x → myopathy
management: Atorvastatin max 10mg/day with cyclosporine. Prefer pravastatin.

mechanism: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2 → theophylline AUC increases 1.5-3x → seizures, arrhythmias
management: Monitor theophylline levels. Reduce theophylline dose 50% or avoid.

mechanism: Disulfiram-like reaction: metronidazole inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase → acetaldehyde accumulation → flushing, vomiting, hypotension
management: ABSOLUTE prohibition of alcohol during metronidazole and for 48 hours after completion.

mechanism: Additive CNS and respiratory depression — synergistic at opioid and GABA-A receptors
management: AVOID combination if possible. If required, use lowest doses. Naloxone available. Monitor respiratory rate.

mechanism: Additive serotonergic effects + CYP2D6 competition → serotonin syndrome risk
management: Avoid combination. If necessary, use lowest doses and monitor for serotonin syndrome (hyperthermia, agitation, clonus).

mechanism: Fluoxetine inhibits CYP2D6 → impairs codeine → morphine conversion → reduced analgesia AND accumulates norcodeine → CNS effects
management: Avoid combination. Use alternative analgesic not dependent on CYP2D6 (e.g. paracetamol, NSAIDs).

mechanism: Clarithromycin MBI of CYP3A4 → fentanyl levels increase dramatically → respiratory depression
management: Avoid or reduce fentanyl dose 50-75%. Intensive monitoring. Consider opioid rotation.

mechanism: Combined neurotoxicity — irreversible brain damage reported at high doses; additive EPS and QT prolongation
management: Use lowest effective doses. Monitor lithium levels and neurological status closely.

mechanism: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2 → clozapine levels increase 2-3x → seizures, agranulocytosis risk
management: Monitor clozapine levels closely. Reduce clozapine dose or use alternative antibiotic (avoid fluoroquinolones).

mechanism: Carbamazepine is potent CYP3A4 inducer → quetiapine AUC reduced 85-90%
management: 5-fold dose increase required. Prefer alternative mood stabilizer/anticonvulsant if possible.

mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandins → reduce renal lithium clearance → lithium toxicity
management: Avoid NSAIDs with lithium if possible. Use paracetamol instead. If unavoidable, monitor lithium level closely.

mechanism: ACE inhibitors reduce angiotensin II → decreased aldosterone → sodium loss → kidney compensates by retaining lithium
management: Monitor lithium levels within 1 week of starting/changing ACE inhibitor dose.

mechanism: Loop diuretics cause sodium depletion → kidney reabsorbs more lithium to conserve sodium → lithium toxicity
management: Monitor lithium levels after starting diuretic. Maintain adequate sodium/fluid intake.

mechanism: Valproate inhibits UGT1A4 → lamotrigine glucuronidation reduced → lamotrigine levels double
management: When adding valproate to lamotrigine, halve lamotrigine dose. Titrate slowly.

mechanism: Carbapenems inhibit VPA tubular reabsorption and increase glucuronidation → VPA levels drop 50-90% within 24hr
management: AVOID combination if valproate essential. If carbapenem required, switch to alternative anticonvulsant (levetiracetam, phenobarbital). Monitor VPA levels daily.

mechanism: Carbamazepine induces CYP2C9 → warfarin metabolism accelerated → INR falls
management: Increase warfarin dose (may need to double). Monitor INR weekly during carbamazepine initiation/dose changes.

mechanism: Carbamazepine induces CYP3A4 → OCP metabolism accelerated → contraceptive failure risk
management: Use non-hormonal contraception or depot medroxyprogesterone. If OCP used, higher-dose pill + barrier method.

mechanism: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 → reduced clopidogrel activation → reduced antiplatelet effect
management: Use pantoprazole (weaker CYP2C19 inhibition) instead of omeprazole/esomeprazole.

mechanism: Additive anticoagulation: LMWH anti-Xa effect + aspirin platelet inhibition + GI mucosal damage → bleeding
management: Combination used clinically (ACS) — ensure benefits outweigh bleeding risk. Add PPI for GI protection.

mechanism: Additive serotonergic effect → serotonin syndrome; also CYP2D6 competition
management: Avoid combination. Use non-serotonergic analgesic.

mechanism: Bupropion inhibits CYP2D6 → tamoxifen active metabolite endoxifen levels reduced → reduced breast cancer efficacy
management: AVOID bupropion in breast cancer patients on tamoxifen. Use alternative antidepressant (venlafaxine, mirtazapine).

mechanism: Bupropion inhibits CYP2D6 → impaired codeine activation → reduced analgesia
management: Use alternative analgesic not dependent on CYP2D6 activation.

mechanism: Linezolid is MAO inhibitor + serotonin reuptake inhibition → additive → serotonin syndrome
management: CONTRAINDICATED. Washout SSRIs before linezolid. If urgent linezolid needed, stop SSRI and wait 2 weeks (or 5 for fluoxetine).

mechanism: Additive nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity — both can cause tubular damage
management: Monitor renal function and drug levels closely. Ensure adequate hydration.

mechanism: Metoclopramide is dopamine D2 antagonist → directly antagonizes levodopa's central dopaminergic effect
management: AVOID metoclopramide in Parkinson's disease. Use domperidone (does not cross BBB) for nausea.

mechanism: Fluoxetine strongly inhibits CYP2D6 → tamoxifen → endoxifen conversion blocked → reduced breast cancer protection
management: AVOID fluoxetine/paroxetine with tamoxifen. Use venlafaxine or citalopram for hot flashes/depression.

mechanism: Additive DHFR inhibition → severe myelosuppression (pancytopenia) — both inhibit folate pathway
management: CONTRAINDICATED in high-dose MTX. In low-dose RA MTX: avoid TMP-SMX if alternatives exist; if unavoidable monitor CBC closely.

mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal tubular secretion of MTX → MTX accumulates → myelosuppression/mucositis
management: Avoid NSAIDs, especially perioperatively. Use paracetamol. If NSAID required, hold MTX 24hr before/after and monitor closely.

mechanism: Carbamazepine induces CYP3A4 → imatinib AUC reduced 70% → loss of CML/GIST efficacy
management: Avoid carbamazepine. Switch to levetiracetam or other non-inducing anticonvulsant. If unavoidable, increase imatinib dose.

mechanism: Testosterone increases warfarin effect via unclear mechanism (possibly reduced protein binding + direct hepatic effects)
management: Monitor INR closely when starting/stopping testosterone. Warfarin dose reduction likely needed.

mechanism: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2 → theophylline clearance reduced 30-50%
management: Reduce theophylline dose 30-50% or monitor levels closely. Use azithromycin if possible.
